1: A revelation of God: "you who pass judgment on someone else, for at whatever point you judge another, you are condemning yourself." The diatribe turns from the pagan to the moralist — whether Gentile philosopher or Torah-observant Jew. The logic is inexorable: one who judges by the same moral standard one violates provides the criteria for one's own condemnation. No one stands outside the need for the righteousness Christ provides.
2: A revelation of God: "God's judgment against those who do such things is based on truth." The standard is not social convention or self-assessed virtue but the objective truth of who God is and what he requires. This grounding of judgment in divine truth is what makes the gospel of imputed righteousness the only solution — only a righteousness grounded in God's own truth can meet this standard.
3: A revelation of God: "do you think you will escape God's judgment?" — rhetorical question to the moralizing judge who applies standards he himself fails. The assumption of exemption — whether through religious identity, moral effort, or ritual observance — is demolished here as preparation for the announcement that the only escape is through Christ (Romans 5:1-2: "justified through faith, we have peace with God").
4: A revelation of God: "God's kindness is intended to lead you to repentance." The patience of God is not indifference to sin but a summons — the classical OT characterization of God as slow to anger (Exodus 34:6; Joel 2:13) is here given a teleological shape: divine forbearance aims at repentance. Paul will later argue that it is Christ's death that absorbs the wrath which patience deferred (Romans 3:25-26).
5: A revelation of God: "the day of God's wrath, when his righteous judgment will be revealed" — the eschatological day of judgment that the prophets announced (Zephaniah 1:14-18) is here established as certain. Paul's letter moves between the present revelation of wrath (Romans 1:18) and its future eschatological completion; Christ's intercession (Romans 8:34) is what stands between the believer and that day.
6: A revelation of God: "God will repay each person according to what they have done" — citing Psalms 62:12 and Proverbs 24:12. Individual moral accountability before a perfectly just God is an axiom Paul affirms, not softens. The gospel does not abolish this principle but fulfills it: Christ bears what the repaid verdict requires for those united to him, so that the "repayment" of wrath falls on him rather than on them.
7: A theme: "to those who by persistence in doing good seek glory, honor and immortality, he will give eternal life." The category of those who actually do good consistently enough to inherit eternal life by that route is, Paul will demonstrate (Romans 3:10-12), empty. But the category itself is genuine: the standard is real. Christ is the only one who actually meets it, and by faith, those in him receive the verdict they could not earn.
8: A revelation of God: wrath and anger for those who "reject the truth and follow evil" — the judicial response to persistent truth-rejection is not arbitrary but proportionate to the gravity of suppressing what God has made plain (Romans 1:19-20). The revelation of wrath (Romans 1:18) is now given its concrete judicial content: future reckoning for sustained defection from truth.
9: A revelation of God: "trouble and distress for every human being who does evil: first for the Jew, then for the Gentile." The priority order of Romans 1:16 ("first to the Jew") applies equally to judgment: the covenant privilege that makes Israel's blessing first also makes Israel's accountability first. This is not anti-Jewish but covenantal — greater privilege entails greater accountability (Amos 3:2).
10: A revelation of God: "glory, honor and peace for everyone who does good: first for the Jew, then for the Gentile." The symmetry with v9 reinforces Paul's point about God's impartiality: both the blessing and the curse of the Deuteronomic covenant apply universally. The ground of the promised "peace" (shalom) that Paul will announce in Romans 5:1 ("we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ") is established here as something God gives — not something humanity produces.
11: A revelation of God: "God does not show favoritism" — prosōpolēmpsia (partiality, literally "receiving of faces"). This principle, drawn from Deuteronomy 10:17, is foundational for Paul's argument that the gospel is for all without distinction (Romans 3:22-23). The impartial God who judges impartially also justifies impartially — "to all who believe" (Romans 3:22), regardless of ethnic or religious identity.
12: A revelation of God: those without the law perish apart from the law; those under it are judged by it. The universality of accountability is preserved across both tracks. This sets up the devastating point Paul is building to: no track — Gentile conscience or Jewish Torah — actually produces the righteousness God requires. Both tracks lead to the same conclusion: the need for what Romans 3:21-24 provides.
13: A revelation of God: "it is not those who hear the law who are righteous in God's sight, but it is those who obey the law who will be declared righteous." The standard is genuine obedience, not mere possession or knowledge. Paul will shortly demonstrate (Romans 3:9-18) that no one meets this standard — establishing that the "declaration of righteousness" must come from somewhere other than law-observance. Christ is the one who fully obeys and whose obedience is reckoned to faith (Romans 5:19).
14: A revelation of God: Gentiles who "do by nature things required by the law" demonstrate that the moral structure of God's law is not foreign to humanity but inscribed in it — the image of God (Genesis 1:26-27) is not wholly erased. This partial witness anticipates the new covenant's full work: writing the law on the heart by the Spirit (Jeremiah 31:33; Ezekiel 36:26-27), which Paul will later describe as fulfilled in those who walk by the Spirit (Romans 8:4).
15: A shadow: the law "written on their hearts" in conscience is a partial and unreliable anticipation of the new covenant promise of Jeremiah 31:33, where God himself writes his law on the heart fully and effectively. Paul's language here deliberately echoes Jeremiah's new covenant passage; the conscience is the shadow, the Spirit's work in the new covenant is the substance. The shadow establishes accountability; the substance is what Christ's work makes possible.
16: A direct declaration: "the day when God judges people's secrets through Jesus Christ." The eschatological judgment — already established in vv5-6 — is here identified as mediated through Christ. The one who died for sinners is also the one who will judge them; the gospel and the judgment have the same Christ at their center. This is the only explicit mention of Christ in this chapter, placed at its structural climax: all of Paul's argument about sin and judgment converges on the one through whom God will adjudicate.
17: A revelation of God: the diatribe now addresses directly the Jew who relies on the law and boasts in God. Paul is not critiquing Jewish identity as such but the assumption that covenant privilege functions as a shield against the universal accountability he has established. The critique is the prophetic tradition's own (Micah 3:11; Jeremiah 7:4): claiming God without corresponding obedience.
18: A revelation of God: "you know his will and approve of what is superior because you are instructed by the law." The law's genuine advantage — giving knowledge of God's will — is acknowledged, not denied. But knowledge of the superior without corresponding practice deepens rather than reduces culpability. Paul will argue in Romans 7 that the law cannot produce what it reveals to be necessary.
19: A revelation of God: "you are convinced that you are a guide for the blind, a light for those who are in the dark." Paul echoes the Isaianic Servant language (Isaiah 42:6-7) applied now to the Jewish teacher's self-understanding. The irony is severe: Israel was given the Servant's mission-language, but the Servant himself has now come and been rejected. The teachers of the blind are themselves blind (John 9:39-41).
20: A revelation of God: "instructor of the foolish, a teacher of little children, because you have in the law the embodiment of knowledge and truth." The law is genuinely the embodiment of knowledge and truth — Paul does not dispute this. But the one who possesses the embodiment while failing its demands is in a worse position than the one who never had it. Privilege is responsibility.
21: A revelation of God: "you, then, who teach others, do you not teach yourself?" The rhetorical questions are from the prophetic tradition (Psalms 50:16-21; Micah 3:11) — the gap between claimed identity and actual conduct is Israel's persistent failure, which the prophets indicted across centuries. Paul reprises the indictment not to condemn Israel uniquely but to establish that no ethnic or religious category provides exemption from the universal need for grace.
22: A revelation of God: "you who say people should not commit adultery, do you commit adultery?" The questions intensify: stealing, adultery, robbing temples. The specific transgressions suggest Paul may be drawing on known patterns rather than rhetorical invention. The point is structural: moral knowledge without moral power is not righteousness but heightened condemnation. What is needed is not better instruction but a new nature — what Christ accomplishes through the Spirit.
23: A revelation of God: "you who boast in the law, do you dishonor God by breaking the law?" Boasting in the law while breaking it is the precise form of self-deception Paul dismantles. The solution is not better law-keeping but the righteousness that comes through faith (Romans 3:22) — a righteousness external to the self, received rather than achieved, which is what the boasting of 3:27 will be excluded from claiming.
24: A revelation of God: Paul quotes Isaiah 52:5 LXX — "God's name is blasphemed among the Gentiles because of you." In Isaiah the charge falls on Israel in exile; Paul applies it to Torah-breaking Jews in the diaspora. The name of God, which Israel was called to make known among the nations, is dishonored by the conduct of those who bear it. Christ, in contrast, is the one through whom God's name is rightly glorified among the Gentiles (Romans 15:9).
25: A theme: "circumcision has value if you observe the law" — Paul engages the foundational covenant sign of Gen 17 not to abolish it but to expose the conditionality built into its own terms. Genesis 17:14 specifies excision for the uncircumcised who breaks the covenant; circumcision without covenant-keeping is self-undermining. This argument prepares for the redefinition Paul will offer in vv28-29 and develop fully in Romans 4 (Abraham's faith before circumcision).
26: A revelation of God: "if those who are not circumcised keep the law's requirements, will they not be regarded as though they were circumcised?" The hypothetical raises the question of what circumcision actually signifies. Paul's answer will be Spirit-worked inner transformation (v29), which is the new covenant fulfillment of what the external sign always pointed toward. The uncircumcised Gentile who obeys by the Spirit exceeds the circumcised Jew who violates by the flesh.
27: A revelation of God: "the one who is not circumcised physically and yet obeys the law will condemn you who, even though you have the written code and circumcision, are a lawbreaker." The possession of the external sign without the internal reality inverts the anticipated order of privilege and judgment. Paul's logic here anticipates the new covenant community: what matters is not the sign but the reality the sign was designed to represent.
28: A revelation of God: "a person is not a Jew who is one only outwardly." Paul does not abolish Jewish identity but redefines its essence. The external markers — circumcision, law, ethnic descent — are not the core; they are the outer form of an inner reality. This prepares the radical claim of v29 and the redefinition of "Israel" that Paul will return to in Romans 9-11.
29: A fulfillment: "circumcision is circumcision of the heart, by the Spirit, not by the written code." Paul explicitly fulfills the new covenant promise of Jeremiah 31:33 ("I will put my law in their minds and write it on their hearts") and the Deuteronomic demand of Deuteronomy 30:6 ("the Lord your God will circumcise your hearts"). What Moses foresaw and Jeremiah promised, the Spirit of Christ effects in those who believe. The "true Jew" is defined by the Spirit's work — the eschatological fulfillment has arrived.
